skill.name: native-data-fetching
skill.description: Guides Expo and React Native developers through fetch-based API calls, React Query setup, authentication, offline handling, and environment-based API configuration.
skill.summary: Implement reliable data fetching patterns for Expo apps.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical combination of code execution, network access, and credential access, but review found only Markdown documentation and fenced TypeScript examples. The backtick findings are false positives from template literals and code fences; network and environment references are expected guidance for Expo data fetching.

low_findings.title: External command findings are false positives
low_findings.description: The scanner interpreted TypeScript template literals and fenced Markdown blocks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Review found documentation examples only, with no executable script, shell invocation, or command injection path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines are inside SKILL.md documentation or TypeScript code fences. No evidence found of runtime shell execution or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Network examples are expected documentation
low_findings.description: The skill intentionally teaches fetch-based API access for Expo apps. The detected URLs are example, localhost, or placeholder production endpoints, and the skill does not make network requests itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static network matches align with the skill description and appear in instructional snippets. No evidence found of hidden callbacks, telemetry, or exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Environment variable examples are documented safely
low_findings.description: The skill discusses Expo public environment variables and .env variants for API configuration. It explicitly warns users not to put secrets in EXPO_PUBLIC_ variables and separates server-side secrets from client-safe values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment references are explanatory examples and security notes. No evidence found that the skill reads local environment files or attempts to collect secrets.

low_findings.title: Critical combination heuristic is dismissed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined false-positive command execution with expected network and environment examples. Review found no malicious intent, no prompt injection attempt, and no executable credential exfiltration path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review confirms the signals occur in normal teaching material. No evidence found of instructions to bypass the audit, contact unauthorized services, or expose private credentials.

low_findings.title: Weak crypto and reconnaissance findings are false positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged front matter and an axios preference line, but the file contains no cryptographic algorithm and no host, system, or package reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive text about API calls and a preference to avoid axios. No evidence found of crypto implementation or system inspection commands.

content.value_statement: Expo apps need predictable API calls, caching, authentication, and offline behavior. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for native data fetching.
actual_capabilities: Shows fetch-based GET and POST request patterns for Expo apps.
Explains React Query setup, queries, mutations, caching, and invalidation.
Covers typed error handling, retries, cancellation, and network failures.
Guides token storage with expo-secure-store and authenticated request wrappers.
Documents offline status handling with NetInfo and React Query online state.
Explains Expo public environment variables for API URL configuration.
limitations: Provides guidance and examples, not a runnable package or library.
Uses placeholder endpoints that must be replaced with real project APIs.
Does not implement backend API routes or server-side authentication flows.
Does not verify that copied examples match every app architecture.

use_cases.title: Add API calls to an Expo screen
use_cases.description: Create fetch wrappers, loading states, and response checks for a mobile feature.

use_cases.title: Standardize app-wide data caching
use_cases.description: Choose React Query defaults, invalidation rules, and offline behavior for a growing app.

use_cases.title: Review authentication request patterns
use_cases.description: Check token storage, bearer headers, refresh behavior, and public environment settings.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic request
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single API call.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use native-data-fetching to add a fetch-based GET request for this Expo screen. Include loading, error, and empty states.

prompt_templates.title: Add mutation handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use native-data-fetching to implement a POST request for this form. Add response validation and user-friendly error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Introduce React Query
prompt_templates.scenario: Caching and invalidation for shared data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use native-data-fetching to refactor these API calls into React Query queries and mutations with clear cache keys.

prompt_templates.title: Design resilient networking
prompt_templates.prompt: Use native-data-fetching to design an authenticated Expo data layer with token storage, refresh handling, retries, cancellation, and offline status support.

output_examples.input: User asks how to call a user profile endpoint from an Expo screen.
output: A recommended fetch wrapper with status checks.
A screen flow covering loading, failure, and successful profile display.
Notes about replacing placeholder URLs with project configuration.

output_examples.input: User asks whether to use React Query or a custom hook.
output: A short comparison based on cache complexity and app size.
A recommendation for React Query when data is shared across screens.
Suggested cache keys, stale time, and invalidation points.

output_examples.input: User asks where to store an API token in an Expo app.
output: Guidance to store sensitive tokens in expo-secure-store.
A warning against client-exposed public environment variables for secrets.
A request wrapper plan that adds bearer headers only when a token exists.

best_practices: Check response status before parsing successful data.
Keep client-visible configuration limited to EXPO_PUBLIC_ values that are not secret.
Use SecureStore for tokens and React Query for shared cached server state.
anti_patterns: Do not store authentication tokens in AsyncStorage.
Do not put private API keys or database credentials in EXPO_PUBLIC_ variables.
Do not ignore cancellation, retry limits, or offline states in mobile requests.

faq.question: Does this skill make network requests by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown guidance and examples for implementing requests in an Expo app.

faq.question: Does it require axios?
faq.answer: No. The skill recommends fetch-based patterns and mentions axios only as a less preferred option.

faq.question: Can it help with React Query?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers provider setup, queries, mutations, cache invalidation, and offline status integration.

faq.question: Can I use EXPO_PUBLIC_ variables for secrets?
faq.answer: No. EXPO_PUBLIC_ variables are visible in the client bundle and should only hold client-safe values.

faq.question: Does it cover offline behavior?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains NetInfo status checks and React Query online manager integration.

faq.question: Is this a complete API client library?
faq.answer: No. It is an instructional skill that helps an AI assistant generate project-specific data fetching code.
